verses.commentary: The Lord appointed seventy-two others (some manuscripts: seventy) and sent them two by two ahead of him to every town and place where he was about to come. Lukan unique — wider sending beyond the Twelve, symbolizing the nations (Genesis 10 — 70 or 72 nations).
'The harvest is plentiful, but the laborers are few. Pray earnestly to the Lord of the harvest to send out laborers.' Prayer before recruitment.
'Go your way; I send you out as lambs in the midst of wolves.' Vulnerability assigned. The metaphor warns and frames.
'Carry no moneybag, no knapsack, no sandals; greet no one on the road.' Total focus. Even social greetings are bypassed for mission speed.
Entering a house, first say: 'Peace be to this house.' Shalom-blessing as opening word.
If a son of peace is there, your peace will rest on him; if not, it returns to you. Peace is reactive — to receptivity.
Remain in the same house, eating and drinking what they provide — the laborer deserves his wages. Do not move from house to house. Stable base, hospitality-respectful.
When you enter a town and they receive you, eat what is set before you. Even Jewish food laws are flexed for mission — significant precedent (compare Acts 10).
Heal the sick and say to them: 'The kingdom of God has come near to you.' Healing and proclamation paired.
When they do not receive you — go into the streets and say:
'Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet we wipe off against you. Nevertheless know this: the kingdom of God has come near.' Judgment-marker and kingdom-announcement together.
'It will be more bearable on that day for Sodom than for that town.' Sodom less culpable. Refusal of the gospel exceeds even Sodom's offense.

'Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the mighty works done in you had been done in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.' Gentile cities would have responded better.
Tyre and Sidon will fare better than these towns at judgment.
'And you, Capernaum — will you be exalted to heaven? You will be brought down to Hades.' Echo of Isaiah 14:13-15 (the king of Babylon's pride). Jesus' base city receives the harshest woe.
'Whoever hears you hears me; whoever rejects you rejects me; whoever rejects me rejects him who sent me.' Apostolic authority chained to the Father through Christ.
Seventy-two return with joy: 'Lord, even the demons are subject to us in your name.' Mission success reported with delight.
'I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.' Apocalyptic vision granted. Their exorcisms are cosmic-scale events; each is Satan's local defeat.
'I have given you authority to tread on serpents and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy; nothing shall hurt you.' Psalm 91:13 echo. Eschatological protection.
'Nevertheless do not rejoice that the spirits are subject to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.' Better cause for joy: secure status, not transient performance.

In that hour Jesus rejoiced (egalliasato, ἠγαλλιάσατο) in the Holy Spirit (Lukan distinctive): 'I thank you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, that you have hidden these things from the wise and understanding and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for such was your gracious will.' Trinitarian joy explicit.

'All things have been handed over to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, or who the Father is except the Son and anyone to whom the Son chooses to reveal him.' Mutual Father-Son knowledge — revelation flows through the Son.
Turning to disciples privately: 'Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.' Privileged historical moment.
Many prophets and kings desired to see what you see and did not, to hear what you hear and did not. The disciples occupy the eschatological waiting-list answered.
A lawyer (nomikos, νομικός) stood up to test (ekpeirazon, ἐκπειράζων) Jesus: 'Teacher, what shall I do to inherit eternal life?' Standard rabbinic question, hostile intent.
'What is written in the Law? How do you read it?' Returns the question. Lawyer must declare his interpretation.
Cites Deuteronomy 6:5 (love God totally) and Leviticus 19:18 (love neighbor as yourself). Standard Jewish summary.
'You have answered correctly. Do this, and you will live.' Diagnostic — doing, not just knowing.
Wanting to justify himself: 'And who is my neighbor?' Self-justification through restrictive definition. The question reveals the heart.

Parable of the Good Samaritan begins. A man going down from Jerusalem to Jericho — descending through wilderness, robber-territory. Beaten, stripped, left half-dead. Anonymous victim — no ethnic identification given.

Priest going down — sees, passes by on the other side. Religious caste avoids the suffering man. Possible Levitical concern about corpse-defilement (if he assumed the man was dead) — but the result is failure of love.
Levite same — sees, passes by on the other side. Second priestly caste, same response. Pattern established.
A Samaritan, as he journeyed, came to where he was. Saw him, had compassion (esplagchnisthe, ἐσπλαγχνίσθη). The despised half-Jew shows mercy where the ethnically pure walked past.
Goes to him, binds his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Sets him on his own beast. Brings him to an inn and cares for him. Multiple costly acts.
Next day, two denarii (about two days' wages) to the innkeeper: 'Care for him; whatever more you spend, I will repay when I return.' Open-ended financial commitment.
'Which of these three proved to be a neighbor to the man who fell among robbers?' Jesus reverses the question. The lawyer asked who deserves neighbor-status; Jesus asks who acted as neighbor.

Lawyer (cannot bring himself to say 'Samaritan'): 'The one who showed him mercy.' Jesus: 'Go and do likewise.' From identification to imitation. The neighbor-question becomes a discipleship-imperative.
On the journey he entered a village. A woman named Martha received him into her house. Hospitality given.
Her sister Mary sat at the Lord's feet, listening to his teaching. Rabbinic learning posture — radical for a woman in first-century Judaism.
Martha distracted with much serving (diakonian, διακονίαν). Approaches: 'Lord, do you not care that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her to help me.' Frustration delivered as theology.
'Martha, Martha, you are anxious and troubled about many things.' Tender address — the doubled name signals affection.

'But one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion, which will not be taken from her.' Singular focus on Christ-as-teacher is the higher calling. Service has its place; receiving comes first.
